questions.question: A map has a scale of $1 : n$. A rectangular field measuring $150\text{ m}$ by $100\text{ m}$ has an area of $6\text{ cm}^2$ on the map. Find the value of $n$.
questions.explanation:
Actual Area = $150 \times 100 = 15\,000\text{ m}^2$. Area scale ratio is $6\text{ cm}^2 : 15\,000\text{ m}^2 \implies 1\text{ cm}^2 : 2500\text{ m}^2$. Take the square root to get the linear scale ratio: $1\text{ cm} : \sqrt{2500}\text{ m} \implies 1\text{ cm} : 50\text{ m}$. Convert $50\text{ m}$ to cm: $50 \times 100 = 5000\text{ cm}$. Thus, $n = 5000$.

**Common mistake:** Converting square meters to square centimeters incorrectly, or forgetting to square root^2500 to find linear scale.

questions.question: On a map of scale $1 : 75\,000$, a nature reserve has an area of $32\text{ cm}^2$. Calculate the area of the same nature reserve on an aerial photograph where the linear scale is $1\text{ cm}$ to $300\text{ m}$.
questions.explanation:
Map scale: $1\text{ cm} = 75\,000\text{ cm} = 750\text{ m}$. Photo scale: $1\text{ cm} = 300\text{ m}$. The ratio of linear scales between the map and photograph is $750 / 300 = 2.5$. Since the photograph has a larger scale (more detail), the area will be larger by a factor of $2.5^2 = 6.25$. Photo area = $32 \times 6.25 = 200\text{ cm}^2$.

**Common mistake:** Multiplying by the linear ratio $2.5$ instead of the squared area ratio $6.25$.

questions.question: The scale of a map is $1 : 250\,000$. A circular lake has a radius of $1.4\text{ cm}$ on the map. Find the actual area of the lake to the nearest square kilometer ($\text{km}^2$). (Take $\pi = \frac{22}{7}$)
questions.explanation:
Linear scale factor: $1\text{ cm} : 2.5\text{ km}$. Actual radius of lake = $1.4 \times 2.5 = 3.5\text{ km}$. Actual Area = $\pi r^2 = \frac{22}{7} \times 3.5 \times 3.5 = 22 \times 0.5 \times 3.5 = 11 \times 3.5 = 38.5\text{ km}^2$.

**Common mistake:** Calculating map area first ($\pi \times 1.4^2 = 6.16\text{ cm}^2$) and then multiplying by $250\,000$ linearly instead of multiplying by $2.5^2$.

questions.question: A physical region with an area of $180\text{ km}^2$ is represented on a map by a sector of a circle with radius $12\text{ cm}$ and sector angle $40^\circ$. Taking $\pi = \frac{22}{7}$, find the scale of the map in the form $1 : n$.
questions.explanation:
Map sector area $= \frac{40}{360} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 12^2 = \frac{352}{7}\text{ cm}^2$. Compare the area ratio with the actual area to find the linear scale: if $1\text{ cm}$ represents $3\text{ km}$, then $1\text{ cm}^2$ represents $9\text{ km}^2$, so the map area is $180 \div 9 = 20\text{ cm}^2$. This gives $n = 300\,000$.

**Common mistake:** Students use the linear scale factor instead of its square when converting between map area and actual area.

questions.question: On a map of scale $1 : n$. $x\text{ cm}$ and an area of $A\text{ cm}^2$. If both the map linear dimensions are doubled, what are the new map length and new map area expressions?
questions.explanation:
Doubling all linear dimensions implies that lengths are scaled by a factor of 2 ($2x$. $2^2 = 4$), resulting in $4A$.

**Common mistake:** Thinking that area also doubles ($2A$) when linear components are doubled.
